tweets.text:
Good morning.
My three takeaways from China’s latest inflation data:
Annual PPI inflation accelerated to 4.1% in June, the highest in four years and up from 3.9% in May (see FT chart below).
With annual CPI softening to 1.0% (down from 1.2%), the gap between wholesale costs and consumer prices widened.
On a sequential basis, monthly PPI actually contracted by 0.3% in June, largely reflecting the sharp pullback in international oil prices.
#economy #china #inflation #markets @FT

With the US and Iran now trading blows, there were no US escorts in the last 24 hours. Inbound VLCCs unrelated to Iran fell to zero for the last 2 days.

The key to the oil math was always inbound tankers, not outbound tankers. With no incoming VLCCs, production shut-in will rise, which is the essence of the oil market deficit.

As you will see in the @MarineTraffic video, AIS spoofing appears about halfway through. According to @MenchOsint 's reporting today, IRGC speed boats turned vessels around in the Oman lane. This will also prevent shadow transits from taking place.

With US conducting the largest strike since the ceasefire took place in April, it will be interesting to see what develops further.

I have included my inbound tanker list below for the last 3 days.

Jeremy Giffon on why the best investment ideas are simple, and the legendary Richard Rainwater's "one-pager" test:

 "A lot of investors are in the feel clever, look smart game more than the money game.

You either have to say, i'm looking for ideas so complex that no one is going to do them, or it should actually be quite simple.

I'm a bigger believer in simplicity, which is you want to be long Elon.

Or you should just buy big companies when they're at their 200-week moving average. 

I love that idea because it's just so simple. But it's right. The gift is being able to sell that idea. 

One story that I absolutely love on cutting through the mess and getting to complete clarity on an investment is from Richard Rainwater.

You would come into his office with a yellow legal pad, and you would write out your thesis on one page, and then you would tell him what percentage of your net worth you're gonna put in the deal. 

Based on your one-page thesis and the percentage of net worth, he would say yes or no."

Financial conditions are easing despite the recent increase in inflation.

tweets.text:
Tech stocks are experiencing one of their most volatile periods in history:

The ratio of the Nasdaq 100 volatility index, $VXN, to the S&P 500 volatility index, $VIX, is up to 1.7 points, the highest in 23 years.

This marks the first time since 2018 that the ratio has surpassed 1.5 points.

By comparison, during the 2008 Financial Crisis, this metric peaked at ~1.6 points.

This comes as $VXN stands at 28 points while the $VIX is at 16 points, or 43% lower.

$VXN has now been above 20 points for 5 consecutive months, the longest streak since the 2022 bear market.

The market is pricing-in severe volatility in tech.

This is why virtually every major technology company is racing to build AI agents.

The next phase of the AI Revolution has arrived.

tweets.text:
As a VC, Fable is better than me at VC work. 

It breaks down white papers better than I do. It's better at financial analysis. It chews through mountains of diligence in minutes. There is no single well-scoped intellectual task where I beat a frontier AI. I catch its mistakes sometimes, but it catches mine more often.

If my job is "VC work," then Fable is better than me at my job.

But Fable alone would be a mediocre investor. 

It would quickly pass on all the bunk, sure. But among the set of doable deals, it can't find the one with the je ne sais quoi. It doesn't have the bias toward special people, wild ideas, or risk-taking. Its tweets and thinkpieces are OK, but they're too safe, too obvious. I can prompt it in the right direction, but even then it's not quite right. It's too suggestible. It changes its mind too easily. It's not stubborn, self-assured, irrationally confident about how the future will play out, the way great investors are.

But me + Fable is really good. Better than either of us alone. In fact every investor on our team has leveled up with AI in the same way. We all feel we have more to do now, not less. We feel more capable, not less. 

My theory is that AI disempowers service providers and empowers owners. If you sell your labor by the task, AI is genuinely terrifying. It can accomplish that task cheaper, faster, with more control and less bullshit. But if you own the outcome, if you're using AI to do something for you, then AI is amazing. It's empowering, not disempowering. Make your own logo, design your own website, write your own copy, build your own systems. 

It also means is that you should aim to reorganize yourself in the food chain. If you don't, AI will do it for you. Don't be a service provider, be an owner. If you're building something, you are a beneficiary of AI, all of the incredible gains that AI is enabling will accrue to you too. 

But if you're a tax on people building things, just know that the tax rate on building is rapidly going to 0.

Makes businesses less capital intensive almost certainly. Productivity gains generally mean more output, less capital requires. 

If you need more capital for the same output, you haven't gotten richer or increased productivity. 

That doesn't mean small rounds necessarily though, because as a VC we are funding the displacement process. That displacement may be rapid and violent and capital intensive because of the raw amount of change happening, even if the steady state after the transition won't look like that long term.

tweets.text:
American consumers are becoming increasingly bullish on equities:

The perceived probability among US consumers that US stock prices will be higher 1-year from now increased +2.9 percentage points in June, to 40.9%, the highest since April 2021.

This is also the 3rd-highest reading since the 2020 pandemic recovery.

By age, consumers under 40 are the most optimistic, at 43.1%, the highest since May 2021.

This is followed by the 40-59 and over 59 age groups, both at 40.2%, the highest readings this year.

By income, the perceived probability of higher stock prices rose to 48.3% among those earning over $100,000, the highest since January 2025.

This compares with 38.6% among those earning $50,000-$100,000 and 33.6% among those earning under $50,000, both in-line with the average over the last 2 years.

High-income American consumers are historically bullish.

🇪🇺 Europe’s car industry is going through a real moment of truth as the head of Germany’s auto lobby says job cuts and plant closures are becoming inevitable.

➡️ The VDA is now talking about allowing foreign carmakers to use European factories in order to preserve jobs. If European brands no longer have enough volume to fill their own plants, it may be better to produce Chinese cars in Europe than to leave factories empty which makes sense in the short term. 

⚠️ However it raises a much bigger long-term question: where will the value be captured? In European brands, European batteries, European software and European suppliers? Or by foreign groups that already control a large part of the value chain while Europe mainly keeps the final assembly?

✅ I believe that producing locally can help but it is not enough. The real priority is to rebuild competitiveness with cheaper energy, more efficient factories, simpler regulation, a stronger battery ecosystem, affordable vehicles and the ability to launch attractive models faster.

Europe still has huge strengths in the automotive industry but it has to accept that yesterday’s model is not coming back.

*Link: https://t.co/09kue2UNEz

🇨🇳 #China’s deployment of 800 billion yuan ($118 billion) of ultra-long special treasury bonds on priority projects is expected to help support economic growth through the year, according to a China Securities Journal report on Thursday, citing analysts.

Funding will be allocated to 1,417 projects, targeting technological innovation, Yangtze River ecological restoration, transport links, urban utility tunnels, major water conservation works, higher education upgrades and other priority sectors.

Bond issuance is expected to provide a stable funding base for infrastructure spending and help support economic growth through the remainder of the year, said Lianhe Credit Rating.

Funding could leverage as much as 11.6 trillion yuan of total investment in 2026, helping to support a notable rebound in nationwide fixed-asset investment growth in the second half, said Citic Securities analyst Yang Fan
Local government special bond issuance is set to accelerate in the third quarter, with volume projected to rise about 30% from a year earlier, said Guangfa Securities analyst Wu Qiying.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/xHVniF2FNm

amigo, this is longer than my article

in asia, the best presentations were when i was up close with the community, walking through things step-by-step.  (see attached video)

covered bayc, otherside, apecoin - the apecoin content in particular is covered above 

a dialogue more than a lecture. i leave room for myself to be wrong about things and open to changing my pov. but if i'm right, it's a more potent format to build conviction in an audience.

no apechurch wasn't a part of that presentation. made that decision 2 days ago.  ruminated on it while flying back from asia as apes in shanghai expressed potential in the platform.  dug deeper into the app and saw that it was actually the best performing app in its class on any chain.  a 4M house placement seemed like an obvious move to make.  protocols should double down on winners.

apecoin is still the coin of the realm on apechain and otherside.  my argument here is that it's oversold.  there's still a case to be made for the upside beyond the HTF support level (part ii)

think longing bayc/mayc is perfectly fine.  but looking at the ratio, there's a 20:1 preference toward the nft vs the fungible.  like a good car engine, all the parts need to be a bit more balanced for the system to be at its best.  i think 20:1 is too lopsided.  better if we don't have an achilles heel in our eco.

yes there are dedicated teams across these 3 efforts.  but at times they can also become very interrelated so cross-functional roles across multiple areas are sometimes a better fit.

tweets.text:
Let’s put it this way:

Retail thinks a 30-60% drop is a “falling knife” where bagholders will never recover. 

And they end up panic selling after seeing swiggly line TAs and people comparing valuable chokepoints to memes.

I see it as long term ownership over:

- the next hyperscaler with $NBIS, projecting $7-9B ARR Q4
- 40% of the InP supply chain with $AXTI
- leader of the next optical shift with $SIVE with CW DFB lasers
- leader of US humanoids with $CCXI inside a future trillion dollar theme

And so on… At cheaper valuations. 

While I might do things like lowering margin or hedging with my own portfolio, I’m personally not panicking when something drops if the thesis didn’t change. 

NFA and obviously depends on people’s investing timeframes:

Since a crash would be life changing for people that depend on investing for rent or tuition. (Which is why I personally think  others should do their own DD and choose longs in line with their own risk profile)

But my personal goal is maximizing exposure to the next-gen supercycles before they hit.

I think institutions think the same way as well, which is why there’s a lot of induced volatility along the way to maximize their exposure.

If a thesis ends up correct, the valuation should be reflected in the long run.

Multiple incorrect statements here as a practitioner in that era. 

1) it was never accepted that the density of talent was the same. Paris had an unusually high amount, but still paled in comparison to SF. NYC I have no idea where that's coming from since I don't think anyone believed that 
2) generative AI is a different beast so the skillset isn't fully 1-1 transferable. GenAI certainly has a significantly higher density in SF than those two cities as has pulled away more given both major labs are HQd there

tweets.text:
I remember when at my previous company Doctrine we rewrote our frontend from AngularJS 1.7 to React. It was a huge project and a big decision. It took roughly six months and a team of talented software engineers. 

That’s why the Bun rewrite is so interesting to me. Bun was ~535k lines of Zig. The rewrite produced more than 1M lines of Rust diff! But... it took only 11 days. 

At peak they had around 64 agents running in parallel, across 4 worktrees, with 16 agents per workflow. It generated 6,500+ commits (wow!)

It was also a lot of work because they didn't ask "plz Code go from Zig to Rust" but they created a porting guide, a flow to analyze errors, separate adversarial agents to review diff to find mistakes etc. The human job was not to write the code but more designing and supervising the process aka "managing agents."

IMO it's a big deal. The world is full of important software stuck on old frameworks, old languages, old architectures and old security models because rewriting is too expensive and too risky. 

If this methodology becomes reliable, we can move software to safer languages, better runtimes, cleaner architectures and more secure defaults much faster. 

So much software run legacy COBOL and soon the transition to Rust will be easy.

tweets.text:
Microsoft just released TypeScript 7! It’s much faster.  Often 8x to 12x faster on full builds like VS Code went from 125s to ~10s (11x faster!)

There’s a real world where Python remains the language of ML with PyTorch, JAX etc, but TypeScript becomes the language of AI.

Most AI apps are not training models. They’re calling APIs, orchestrating tools, streaming tokens, managing state, auth, billing, workflows, agents, eval dashboards, and UX. Most companies are using TypeScript for that.

That world is getting much more TypeScript-shaped. Basically, Python trains the models and TypeScript ships the product.
